ops.title: Fix the capt-hook framework rather than contorting hooks/tests around its gaps
ops.body:
When a capt-hook hook can't be written cleanly because the framework lacks something (e.g.
inline tests couldn't populate `evt.tasks` because the mock event had no `session_id`), the
user wants the **framework fixed**, not the hook/test contorted around the limitation.

Concrete example from this work: added `Input(tasks=[...])` to `captain_hook/testing/types.py`
and pre-populated the `tasks` cached_property in `input_to_event`
(`captain_hook/testing/helpers.py`) so task gates test their real block/warn paths.

The rule runs in both directions: when a capt-hook check **false-positives on code you're
writing** (e.g. the verbose-comment block denying a legitimate PEP 723 `# /// script` fence),
never ship a workaround in the target repo (a `sys.path` bridge instead of the blocked
`[tool.uv.sources]` header). Fix capt-hook first — carve-out + regression tests + release —
then land the intended code. User, 2026-07-20, after the fleetlib bridge hack: "fix this in
capt-hook then get rid of the hack here … dont ever do a hack like this again."

**Why:** contorting hooks to dodge a framework gap produces "shitty logic"; the framework is
the user's own (`~/Code/captain-hook`) and is meant to be extended. A workaround in the target
repo outlives the session and normalizes evading the guard instead of correcting it.
**How to apply:** `~/Code/captain-hook` runs hooks from its own checkout via `uv run`, so its
own tests pass immediately; other repos use `uvx capt-hook`, so a framework change must be
released (bump + `v*` tag → PyPI trusted-publishing) before their `uvx capt-hook test` /
`bootstrap.py verify` pick it up. Run the change confirmation past the user — publishing is
outward-facing. See [[declarative-capt-hooks]].
